text: Article 22 of the Constitution of India: Protection against arrest and detention. No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed of the grounds of arrest. Every person who is arrested has the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of his choice. Every arrested person must be produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours of arrest.

Consumer Protection Act 2019: A consumer can file a complaint for defective goods or deficient services. Complaints up to Rs 1 crore go to District Consumer Forum. The seller is liable to replace defective goods, refund the price, or pay compensation. E-commerce platforms are also liable under this Act for products sold through their platform.

Transfer of Property Act 1882: A landlord must return the security deposit within a reasonable time after the tenant vacates. Wrongful withholding of security deposit entitles the tenant to file a civil suit for recovery. The tenant can also approach the Rent Controller under the applicable State Rent Control Act.

Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 Section 78: Stalking — any man who follows a woman or contacts her repeatedly despite disinterest commits stalking. Punishment: first offence up to 3 years, repeat offence up to 5 years imprisonment.

Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005: A woman facing domestic violence can approach a Protection Officer or file a complaint before a Magistrate. She is entitled to a Protection Order, Residence Order, Monetary Relief, and Custody Order. The National Women Helpline is 181.

Right to Information Act 2005: Any citizen can request information from any public authority. The Public Information Officer must respond within 30 days. Fee is Rs 10 per application. Information can be denied only on grounds specified in Section 8.

Motor Vehicles Act 1988: A driver involved in an accident must not flee the scene. They must report the accident to the nearest police station within 24 hours. Fleeing the scene is a criminal offence punishable with imprisonment.

Industrial Disputes Act 1947: An employer cannot terminate an employee without giving 1 month notice or pay in lieu of notice for employees with over 1 year of service. Wrongful termination entitles the employee to approach the Labour Commissioner.
